questions.question:
Consider the diagram shown below where a number of LANs are connected by (transparent) bridges. In order to avoid packets looping through circuits in the graph, the bridges organize themselves in a spanning tree. First, the root bridge is identified as the bridge with the least serial number. Next, the root sends out (one or more) data units to enable the setting up of the spanning tree of shortest paths from the root bridge to each bridge. Each bridge identifies a port (the root port) through which it will forward frames to the root bridge. Port conflicts are always resolved in favour of the port with the lower index value. When there is a possibility of multiple bridges forwarding to the same LAN (but not through the root port), ties are broken as follows: bridges closest to the root get preference and between such bridges, the one with the lowest serial number is preferred.

Consider the spanning tree B1, B5, B3, B4, B2 for the given connection of LANs by bridges, that represents the depth first traversal of the spanning tree of bridges. Let host H1 send out a broadcast ping packet. Which of the following options represents the correct forwarding table on B1?

A. Hosts: H1, H2, H3, H4 - Port: 3; Hosts: H5, H6, H7, H8, H9, H10, H11, H12 - Port: 2
B. Hosts: H3, H4 - Port: 3; Hosts: H5, H6, H7, H8, H9, H10, H11, H12 - Port: 2
C. Hosts: H1, H2 - Port: 1; Hosts: H3, H4 - Port: 3; Hosts: H5, H6, H7, H8, H9, H10, H11, H12 - Port: 2
D. Hosts: H1, H2, H3, H4 - Port: 3; Hosts: H5, H6, H7, H8, H9, H10, H11, H12 - Port: 1
questions.answer: A. Hosts: H1, H2, H3, H4 - Port: 3; Hosts: H5, H6, H7, H8, H9, H10, H11, H12 - Port: 2
questions.to_find: The correct forwarding table on bridge B1 after host H1 sends out a broadcast ping packet

understand.plain: In a network with multiple LANs connected by transparent bridges, bridges build a spanning tree to avoid loops. When a host sends a broadcast packet, bridges learn the sender's location and record it in their forwarding tables. The question asks which forwarding table correctly reflects what bridge B1 (the root bridge) would have after H1's broadcast ping traverses the spanning tree B1-B5-B3-B4-B2.

keywords.explain: A layer-2 device that forwards frames between LANs based on MAC addresses; it learns source addresses automatically from incoming frames and builds a forwarding table.
keywords.example: Bridge B1 sees a frame from H1 arrive on port 3, so it records: H1 is reachable via port 3.

keywords.explain: A protocol that eliminates loops in bridged networks by disabling redundant paths, leaving only a tree-shaped topology for forwarding.
keywords.example: If bridges B1, B2, B3 form a triangle, STP disables one link so only two links remain active.

keywords.explain: A table maintained by each bridge that maps host addresses to the port through which they can be reached.
keywords.example: B1's forwarding table entry: {H3, H4} -> port 3 means frames destined for H3 or H4 should exit via port 3.

keywords.explain: A broadcast packet sent to all hosts; every bridge must forward it out all active ports except the one it arrived on, causing the packet to reach all segments.
keywords.example: H1 sends a broadcast; B1 forwards it out all ports so H5, H6, ... all receive it.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='300' viewBox='0 0 520 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='520' height='300' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><rect x='210' y='20' width='100' height='40' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='47' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B1 (Root)</text><line x1='260' y1='60' x2='100' y2='120' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='260' y1='60' x2='420' y2='120' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='50' y='120' width='100' height='40' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='147' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B5</text><rect x='370' y='120' width='100' height='40' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='420' y='147' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B2</text><line x1='100' y1='160' x2='180' y2='220' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='130' y='220' width='100' height='40' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='247' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B3</text><line x1='180' y1='260' x2='260' y2='260' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='210' y='240' width='100' height='40' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='267' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B4</text><text x='30' y='100' class='sub mut'>H1,H2</text><text x='150' y='100' class='sub mut'>H3,H4</text><text x='400' y='100' class='sub mut'>H5-H12</text><text x='260' y='15' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Spanning Tree: B1-B5-B3-B4 / B1-B2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Spanning tree rooted at B1 (root bridge). B1 connects to B5 on the left branch (which further connects to B3-B4) and B2 on the right branch. H1,H2 are on a LAN near the root; H3,H4 are on B1's port-3 side; H5-H12 are reachable through port 2.

given.aim: Determine the forwarding table that bridge B1 builds after host H1 sends a broadcast ping through the spanning tree B1, B5, B3, B4, B2.

terms.meaning: Bridge with the smallest serial number; acts as the root of the spanning tree and has no root port of its own.
terms.example: If bridges are B1-B5, B1 becomes root since it has serial number 1.
terms.connects: B1's forwarding table maps all hosts in the network to the appropriate outgoing port.

terms.meaning: A broadcast frame sent by H1; every bridge forwards it on all active (non-blocked) ports except the one it arrived on.
terms.example: H1 on LAN-L3 sends broadcast; B1 receives it on port 3, then forwards out ports 1 and 2.
terms.connects: As the broadcast propagates, each bridge records the source (H1) address and port, populating forwarding tables.

terms.meaning: The active subset of links forming a loop-free tree; determined by STP with B1 as root.
terms.example: DFS order B1, B5, B3, B4, B2 describes the spanning tree structure.
terms.connects: Only ports on spanning-tree links are active for forwarding; blocked ports do not learn addresses.

terms.meaning: When a bridge receives a frame, it records the source address and the ingress port in its forwarding table.
terms.example: B1 receives H1's ping on port 3 → records H1 -> port 3.
terms.connects: After H1's broadcast traverses the entire tree, B1 learns the port leading to every host that replied or was reached.

given.plan: Identify B1's port assignments from the spanning tree diagram: port 1 leads to one LAN segment, port 2 leads toward B5/B3/B4 side, port 3 leads toward the LAN with H1/H2/H3/H4. When H1's broadcast propagates, B1 first learns H1's source port (port 3). Reply frames from all remote hosts (H5-H12) arrive at B1 on port 2, and H3/H4 are also reachable via port 3. Compile the full forwarding table from these learned entries.

steps.title: Identify B1's port connections from the spanning tree
steps.formula_raw: Spanning tree: B1 (root) -> B5 (via port 2 of B1) and B1 -> LAN with H1,H2,H3,H4 (via port 3 of B1)
steps.apply: In the given network diagram, bridge B1 is the root. B1's port 3 connects to the LAN segment containing H1, H2, H3, and H4. B1's port 2 connects to the rest of the network (the LAN leading to B5, B2, B3, B4 and hosts H5 through H12). Port 1 of B1 connects directly to a LAN that is part of the spanning tree path but all distant hosts are reachable only through ports 2 or 3.
steps.note: The exact port numbering comes from the diagram where lower port indices are preferred for tie-breaking.

steps.title: Trace the broadcast ping from H1
steps.formula_raw: Source learning: for each frame arriving at bridge on port P with source address S, record S -> P in forwarding table
steps.apply: H1 sends a broadcast on its LAN. B1 receives this frame on port 3 and records: H1 -> port 3. B1 then forwards the broadcast out ports 1 and 2 (all active ports except ingress). Hosts H3, H4 (also on the same side as H1, reachable via port 3) reply, and B1 records them via port 3. All hosts H5 through H12 are on the other side of the spanning tree; their reply frames arrive at B1 via port 2, so B1 records H5, H6, H7, H8, H9, H10, H11, H12 -> port 2. H2 is also on the same LAN segment as H1, so H2 -> port 3 as well.
steps.note: Transparent bridges only learn from source addresses of received frames; the broadcast itself causes all hosts to become reachable.

steps.title: Compile the forwarding table for B1
steps.formula_raw: Forwarding table = {host -> port} entries from address learning
steps.apply:
After H1's broadcast ping:
- Port 3: H1, H2, H3, H4 (all hosts on the LAN segment connected to B1's port 3)
- Port 2: H5, H6, H7, H8, H9, H10, H11, H12 (all hosts reachable through B5, B3, B4, B2 side)

This matches option A: {H1,H2,H3,H4 -> Port 3} and {H5,H6,H7,H8,H9,H10,H11,H12 -> Port 2}.
steps.note: Options B and C are incorrect because they miss H1 and H2 from port 3. Option D incorrectly assigns H1-H4 to port 3 but maps H5-H12 to port 1 instead of port 2.

solution.result: The correct forwarding table on B1 is: H1, H2, H3, H4 -> Port 3; H5, H6, H7, H8, H9, H10, H11, H12 -> Port 2. This corresponds to option A.

questions.formula_note: Transparent bridge address learning: a bridge records the source MAC address and ingress port of each received frame. Spanning tree protocol (IEEE 802.1D) ensures loop-free forwarding by disabling redundant links; after a broadcast, all hosts become known to all bridges in the spanning tree.
